source.title: Hierarchical prediction errors in midbrain and basal forebrain during sensory learning

confidence_rationale: Direct empirical evidence that hierarchical prediction errors are computed at OUTCOME time, in midbrain (low-level PEs about stimulus outcome, putatively dopamine) and basal forebrain (high-level PEs about stimulus probabilities, putatively acetylcholine). This is the most direct biological case for Option A in Q-042 -- the precision-related update fires on observed prediction errors, downstream of outcome arrival. Replicated across two cohorts. Discount because BOLD is an indirect neuromodulator proxy and the claim about DA-vs-ACh assignment is contested.
failure_signatures: If REE moves the running-variance update fully upstream to action selection (pure Option B), the Iglesias reading predicts loss of the post-outcome arm that biology specifically relies on for hierarchical PE computation -- the agent would update precision based on anticipated rather than observed errors, breaking the calibration loop that update_residue's current location preserves.

mapping.source_claim_statement: In a hierarchical Bayesian framework, prediction errors at different levels of the hierarchy may be encoded by different neuromodulators. In an audio-visual associative learning fMRI task, low-level PEs about visual stimulus outcome were reflected in midbrain BOLD (consistent with dopamine), and high-level PEs about stimulus probabilities (i.e., volatility-relevant) were reflected in basal forebrain BOLD (consistent with acetylcholine). Both signals were time-locked to outcome arrival -- the moment the cue reveals what actually happened. Replicated across two independent cohorts.
mapping.ree_translation: Iglesias et al. provide the strongest available empirical case for keeping a precision update at outcome time -- Option A in Q-042. The brain computes hierarchical prediction errors AT outcome arrival and broadcasts them through dedicated neuromodulator systems. Crucially, the high-level (volatility-relevant) PE is the one most directly analogous to ARC-016's precision update -- it tracks how surprising the just-arrived outcome was relative to the agent's prior expectation, and it modulates downstream learning rate. The Iglesias reading argues that REE's current ARC-016 location (inside agent.update_residue(), downstream of outcome arrival) is biologically faithful to where the analogous neuromodulator signal actually fires. Combined with the action-selection-time evidence (Aston-Jones, Frank, Schwartenbeck), this paper is the dual-update reading: biology runs both arms, with the post-outcome arm specifically responsible for the precision update on observed errors.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The neuromodulator assignments (DA = low-level PE, ACh = high-level PE) are based on indirect BOLD inference and partly on the Yu-Dayan framework; later pharmacological work has complicated the clean assignments. The audio-visual learning task is narrow -- the strong claim about hierarchical PE timing may not generalise to all decision domains. The paper does not address what fires AT action selection, only what fires AT outcome.
mapping.source_context: Neuron, 2013. Healthy human adults; computational fMRI; hierarchical Bayesian model fit to behaviour and BOLD; replicated across two independent cohorts.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (Neuron, replicated across two cohorts, methodologically careful Stephan-lab work). Mapping fidelity high (0.74) -- the high-level PE in basal forebrain is the closest available biological analogue of ARC-016's precision update. Transfer risk low-moderate (0.27) -- the post-outcome timing claim transfers cleanly even though the specific neuromodulator assignments are contested.
